summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Application for order pursuant to section 102NA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) – Where mandatory aspects of the provision do not apply – Where there are limited allegations as between the parties – Where the trial is imminent – Application refused

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

PARKER J:

INTRODUCTION

These reasons were delivered ex tempore and have been settled from the transcript. Grammatical errors have been corrected, citations have been added and amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clearer and easier to read.
The application before the Court is the mother's application for an order pursuant to section 102NA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).

BACKGROUND

The parties are engaged in parenting proceedings with respect to their four year old daughter, X, born in 2021. The matter is listed for trial on 11 February 2026, 15 days hence.
The current interim parenting orders, insofar as they relate to parental responsibility, living arrangements and time, provide as follows:
Orders made 16 December 2024

Parental Responsibility
The parties will genuinely consult each other in relation to making a decision as parental responsibility for the child, [X] born [date redacted] 2021 and agree to attend at mediation for that purpose in the event that they cannot satisfactorily agree on the decision to be made for the child.
If the parties cannot reach an agreement at mediation, the father be permitted to make the sole ultimate decision about each major long term issue for the child, however prior to the making that decision the father shall:
Advise the mother in writing of the nature of the decision to be made and matters he is considering affecting the decision;
Invite the mother to provide her succinct written input to that decision within 48 hours of sending the advice in 5(a);
Make the decision having considered the mother’s input, if received in the 48 hour time frame;
Make the decision if no written input is received in the 48-hour time frame;
Inform the mother of his ultimate decision in writing, irrespective of whether the mother provided input or not.

Live with and spend time arrangements

6. That [X] live with the father.

7. That [X] spend time with the mother as follows:
a. Commencing 19 December 2024:
From 5:00pm on Thursday until 8:00am on Monday each alternating weekend; and
From 12:00pm on 31 December 2024 until 12:00pm 4 January 2025; and
At such further and other times as agreed between the parties in writing.

b. Commencing 19 March 2025:
From 5:00pm on Wednesday until 8:00am on Monday each alternating weekend; and
At such further and other times as agreed between the parties in writing.
The parties be at liberty to telephone or Facetime [X] from 5:00pm to 5:30pm ACST on Sunday while she is in the other party’s care, or at such other days or times as agreed between the parties in writing, with the parties to do all acts and things to facilitate the call.
Notwithstanding any other order [X] is to spend time with each of the parents on special occasions set out below as follows:

a. For Christmas in 2024, with the father given the effect of order 7(a).
For New Years in 2024/2025 as per Order 7(a)(ii) herein with the mother.

c. Child’s Birthday
The child spend time with the parent with whom they are not otherwise living on the child’s birthdays as follows:
if such birthday falls on a day that the child attend school then from the conclusion of school until 8:00pm;
if such birthday falls on a day that the child do not attend school, including but not limited to a weekend day, public holiday and/or pupil free day then from 2:00 pm until 9:00am the following morning.

d. Mother’s Day and Father’s Day
With the father from 7:00pm on the Saturday prior to Father’s Day to the commencement of school on Monday following Father’s Day.
With the mother from 7:00pm on the Saturday prior to Mother’s Day to the commencement of school on Monday following Mother’s Day.

e. Easter
With the father in even numbered years from 5:00pm on Maundy Thursday to 2:00pm on Easter Sunday and with the mother from 2:00pm on Easter Sunday to 6:00pm on Easter Monday.
With the mother in odd numbered years from 5:00pm on Maundy Thursday to 2:00pm on Easter Sunday and with the father from 2:00pm on Easter Sunday to 6:00pm on Easter Monday.
...

Orders made 24 July 2025

...
The child [X] born on [date redacted] 2021 spend time with the Mother from 5.00pm this day until 8.00am on 29 July 2025 and thereafter the arrangement for [X] to spend time with the Mother be in accordance with order 7(b) made 16 December 2024 with the first of such occasions to commence Wednesday 6 August 2025.
That [X] spend time with the Respondent Mother if she is not already in her care, each year for her brother, [Y’s], birthday from:
(a) 3:00pm – 8:00pm if on a school day; or

(b) 2:00pm – 8:00am the following morning if a non-school day.

...
The mother has been self-represented since 1 December 2025. She has been represented by four separate solicitors throughout the duration of the proceedings.

APPLICABLE LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Section 102NA of the Act provides as follows:
102NA Mandatory protections for parties in certain cases

(1) If, in proceedings under this Act:
(a) a party (the examining party) intends to cross‑examine another party (the witness party); and

(b) there is an allegation of family violence between the examining party and the witness party; and

(c) any of the following are satisfied:
(i) either party has been convicted of, or is charged with, an offence involving violence, or a threat of violence, to the other party;

(ii) a family violence order (other than an interim order) applies to both parties;

(iii) an injunction under section 68B or 114 for the personal protection of either party is directed against the other party;

(iv) the court makes an order that the requirements of subsection (2) are to apply to the cross‑examination;

then the requirements of subsection (2) apply to the cross‑examination.
(2) Both of the following requirements apply to the cross‑examination:
(a) the examining party must not cross‑examine the witness party personally;

(b) the cross‑examination must be conducted by a legal practitioner acting on behalf of the examining party.

Note 1: This section applies both in the case where the examining party is the alleged perpetrator of the family violence and the witness party is the alleged victim, and in the case where the examining party is the alleged victim and the witness party is the alleged perpetrator.

Note 2: This section does not limit other laws that apply to protect the witness party (for example, section 101 requires the court to forbid the asking of offensive questions and section 41 of the Evidence Act 1995 requires the court to disallow certain questions, such as misleading questions).

Note 3: To avoid doubt, a reference to a party in this section includes a reference to a person who is a party because of the operation of a provision of this Act (for example, sections 92 and 92A, which are about intervening parties). This section only applies to an intervening party if the intervening party is involved in the allegation of family violence, whether as the alleged perpetrator or as the alleged victim.
(3) The court may make an order under subparagraph (1)(c)(iv):
(a) on its own initiative; or

(b) on the application of:
(i) the witness party; or

(ii) the examining party; or
(iii) if an independent children’s lawyer has been appointed for a child in relation to the proceedings—that lawyer.
The Family Violence and Cross-Examination of Parties Scheme is directed at preventing problematic issues such as systems abuse, abuse of power imbalances, oppression, re-traumatisation of parties who have been subjected to family violence, and consequential compromising of evidence.[1]
Section 102NA is not about securing representation for a party,[2] but about protection of parties and the integrity of the Court process and the evidence.

DISCUSSION

In this matter, the mother makes allegations of conduct engaged in by the father which could meet the definition of family violence (as discussed below). There is no criminal conviction or current charge in relation to family violence, and no final family violence order or relevant injunction in place. As such, it is a matter for the Court's discretion as to whether an order is made.

Allegations of family violence

“Family violence” is defined in section 4AB of the Act as follows:
4AB Definition of family violence etc.
(1) For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.

(2) Examples of behaviour that may constitute family violence include (but are not limited to):

(a) an assault; or

(b) a sexual assault or other sexually abusive behaviour; or

(c) stalking; or

(d) repeated derogatory taunts; or

(e) intentionally damaging or destroying property; or

(f) intentionally causing death or injury to an animal; or

(g) economic or financial abuse; or
(i) preventing the family member from making or keeping connections with his or her family, friends or culture; or

(j) unlawfully depriving the family member, or any member of the family member’s family, of his or her liberty.

(2A) For the purposes of paragraph (2)(g), examples of behaviour that might constitute economic or financial abuse of a family member include (but are not limited to) the following:
(a) unreasonably denying the family member the financial autonomy that the family member would otherwise have had, such as by:
(i) forcibly controlling the family member’s money or assets, including superannuation; or

(ii) sabotaging the family member’s employment or income or potential employment or income; or

(iii) forcing the family member to take on a financial or legal liability, or status; or

(iv) forcibly or without the family member’s knowledge, accumulating debt in the family member’s name;

(b) unreasonably withholding financial support needed to meet the reasonable living expenses of the family member, or the family member’s child (including at a time when the family member is entirely or predominantly dependent on the person for financial support);

(c) coercing a family member (including by use of threats, physical abuse or emotional or psychological abuse):

(i) to give or seek money, assets or other items as dowry; or

(ii) to do or agree to things in connection with a practice of dowry;
(d) hiding or falsely denying things done or agreed to by the family member, including hiding or falsely denying the receipt of money, assets or other items, in connection with a practice of dowry.
(3) For the purposes of this Act, a child is exposed to family violence if the child sees or hears family violence or otherwise experiences the effects of family violence.

(4) Examples of situations that may constitute a child being exposed to family violence include (but are not limited to) the child:

(a) overhearing threats of death or personal injury by a member of the child’s family towards another member of the child’s family; or

(b) seeing or hearing an assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family; or

(c) comforting or providing assistance to a member of the child’s family who has been assaulted by another member of the child’s family; or

(d) cleaning up a site after a member of the child’s family has intentionally damaged property of another member of the child’s family; or

(e) being present when police or ambulance officers attend an incident involving the assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family.

The mother gave no evidence at all as to family violence in the affidavit she filed in support of her application for a section 102NA order.
A review of the court file has revealed the following evidence deposed to by the mother:
I am worried that the Father’s unilateral suspension of my time has caused further emotional and psychological harm to [X]. She has enjoyed spending time with me and her brother. [X] would be worried about why she is not seeing us. I am concerned that she may think (or be told to think) that I have abandoned her. I do not know what the Father and/or the paternal grandparents have said to [X] about why she is not spending time with me.[3]
[Mr Abadzhieva] believed her Vaccination status limited her from living a normal life, his reason being she cant go to school or childcare.

...
When he ended our relationship and after signatory of parenting plan, my choice to not vaccinate our daughter became the topic that caused most of our disputes, which he instigated.
i. [Mr Abadzhieva] words “you’ve signed it and when [X] turns 2 im getting her vaccinated, there is nothing you can do about it, no court in Australia will support your anti vax stance!

ii. I endured his threats and emotional abuse and continued to persevere paragraph 19 g. Is a prime example of how firm I am Immunising [X]. I arranged care prior to travel and accommodated [Mr Abadzhieva’s] change of roster.
My Choice in regards to her Vaccination status did not waiver, until following a phone conversation his words “ You don’t have a Choice come Monday its happening with or with out you! I booked her first vaccination appointment at the [B] Medical Centre”. I did not consent, I believe I had no choice. Paragraph 19 h.[4]
(As per the original)
I was relieved upon separation and assumed I was no longer subject to his “coercive controlling behaviours” for example; in addition to being postpartum,
...
On 27th of November 2021 at a residence [C] Street, [Mr Abadzhieva] verbally assaulted me for choosing to leave [X] in my sisters care for a couple of hours instead of him. He aggressively handed a distressed [X] to be best friends sister [Ms D] who witnessed his escalating behaviour. He did not allow me to retrieve [X] from his vehicle.

...

30. On Tuesday 21st November 2023 at 6:15pm;

...
when I indicated that he was being controlling and wasn’t making sense, he became unhinged! His tone got loud and aggressive. ([X] witnessed) Persisting to antagonise me, laughed and said well it was your choice to be an anti vaxing fucking cunt! And now you have a vaccinated child! A neighbour intervened as she feared for our safety and [Mr Abadzhieva] left.[5]

(As per the original)

In summary, the mother alleges emotional abuse in the context of the father failing to support her desire not to vaccinate the child and her refusal to take antibiotics when she was unwell during pregnancy, and alleges that during the relationship and post-separation, the father has called her abusive names, including when discussing parenting issues.
There are also allegations in the mother’s affidavit material with respect to the paternal grandmother. Such allegations do not attract the provisions of section 102NA which relates only to allegations of family violence “between the examining party and the witness party.”
Family reports have been prepared by Panel Family Consultant Ms E on 3 September 2024 and 22 August 2025. Relevant passages of the first report include the following:
In interview, Mr [Abadzhieva] reported that there was no violence in their relationship. He also denied calling Ms [Leeuwenkoek] derogatory names, such as “cunt”, and denied behaving in a demeaning or threatening manner. He stated that it was Ms [Leeuwenkoek] who was demanding and “ambushed” him into engaging in mediation at J Family Services; and that the session ended after only ten minutes given Ms [Leeuwenkoek’s] aggressive behaviour.
Mr [Abadzhieva] had no concerns regarding the child’s risk of violence in Ms [Leeuwenkoek’s] care.
In Affidavit material, Ms [Leeuwenkoek] argues that Mr [Abadzhieva] was abusive and demeaning when discussing parenting issues post-separation (e.g. calling her a “fucking cunt”), and that she feared for her and the child’s safety.
In interview, Ms [Leeuwenkoek] claimed that there was family violence in the relationship, in the form of emotional abuse. She stated that once 20 weeks pregnant, their views changed drastically about what they wanted for [X]. For example, she opposed vaccinations. She stated, “he was the most loving person I ever met”, then at around the 20 week mark, when she refused antibiotics, their relationship began to deteriorate. She stated that the day after the child’s birth, Mr [Abadzhieva] threatened to have the child vaccinated whilst she was still in recovery. She stated that then, 16 days after [X’s] birth, she discovered that her son’s father had only a few days left to live. At the same time, [X] was admitted to the Emergency Room (ER) due to congested lungs and Mr [Abadzhieva] blamed Ms [Leeuwenkoek], given the child was not vaccinated. She stated that after her son’s father passed away, she needed to comfort her son and stepdaughter; however, Mr [Abadzhieva] refused to support this and “made me choose between my newborn and my grieving children, and that’s when the arguments started”.
Ms [Leeuwenkoek] stated that [X] had a urinary tract infection (UTI), and that this was confirmed by the doctor whom she spoke with. When asked why Ms [Leeuwenkoek] raised this in the context of family violence, she stated that this was because she had concerns that Mr [Abadzhieva] failed to attend to the child’s needs when unwell, telling her he was too busy to take her to a medical appointment, rather, relying on the paternal grandmother to do so.
The only reference to family violence in the updated family report is an allegation made by the mother against the paternal grandmother.

Other considerations

The orders sought by the mother at trial include the following:[6]
...
That the parties have joint parental and decision-making responsibility for long term issues relating to the child [X] born [date redacted] 2021 (“the child”).
If the parties cannot reach an agreement, the mother be permitted to make the sole ultimate decision about each major issue for the child however prior to the making of that decision the mother shall:
Advise the father in writing of the nature of the decision to be made and matters she is considering affecting the decision;·
Invite the father to provide his succinct written input to that decision within 48 hours of sending the advice in 3(a);
Make the decision having considered the fathers' input, if received in the 48-hour time frame;
Make the decision if no written input is received in the 48-hour time frame;
Inform the father of her ultimate decision in writing, irrespective of whether the Father provided input or not.

...
If the father resides with the paternal grandmother [Mrs F Abadzhieva] then the child shall live with the mother and spend time with the father from 5:00pm Thursday until 8:00am Monday and each alternate week thereafter.
If the father does not reside with the paternal grandmother [Mrs F Abadzhieva] then the child live with the parties on an equal shared care basis as follows:
In Week 1:
With the Father from 8:00am Monday until 8:00am Wednesday;
With the Mother from 8:00am Wednesday until 8:00am Monday
In Week 2:
With the Father from 8:00am Friday until 8:00am Wednesday;
With the Mother from 8:00am Wednesday until 8:00am Friday;
In the event that the father no longer lives and works within the Adelaide area then the child;
Live with the mother.
Spend time with the father each alternate weekend from conclusion of school Friday (or 5:00pm on a non-school day) until commencement of school on Monday (or 9.30am on a non-school day).
In the event that Father is unable to personally care for [X] for more than 24 hours, the Father do forthwith deliver [X] into the Mother's care for the duration of the Father's absence.

School Holidays
During the short school holidays that the parties spend time with the child in the same rotation as paragraph 6(a) and (b) or paragraph 7(a) and (b) as the case may be.
That during the long school holidays the child live with the mother from conclusion of school in term 4 for twenty one (21) days in 2025 and each alternate year thereafter. Noting the father have the second half of the school holidays and each alternate year thereafter.
That [X] remain in the mother’s care for an extra five (5) nights following the twenty (21) days as a way of make-up time for time lost pursuant to the Orders made on 16 December 2024 (“make up time”).

Special Occasions

Easter
That the child spend Easter 2027 with the father from conclusion of school Maundy Thursday (or 5:00pm on a non-school day) until commencement of school on the following Tuesday (or 5:00pm on a non-school day), and each alternate year thereafter.
That the child spend Easter 2026 with the mother from conclusion of school Maundy Thursday (or 5:00pm on a non-school day) until commencement of school on the following Tuesday (or 5:00pm on a non-school day), and each alternate year thereafter.
Child’s Birthday
The child spend time with the parent who does not have the care of the child on her birthday
On a school day from the conclusion of school until 8:00pm;
On a non-school day from 2:00pm until 8:00am the following day
The child spend time with the Respondent Mother if she is not already in her care,[date redacted] each year for her brother, [Y’s], birthday from:
3:30pm – 8:00pm if on a school day; or
2:00pm – 8:00am the following morning if a non-school day.

Mother’s Day / Father’s Day
That the child shall spend time with the mother from 5.00 pm on the Saturday before Mother's Day until 5.00 pm on Mother's Day.
That the child shall spend time with the father from 5.00 pm on the Saturday before Father's Day until 5.00 pm on Father's Day.

Handover
Until 22 July 2026, Handovers on Monday and Wednesdays occur at [G Childcare Centre,] [Suburb H] until the commencement of Term 3, 22 July 2026.
After 22 July 2026, handovers that do not occur at the school Monday, Wednesday, Thursday and Friday occur at a McDonald's Restaurant, [Suburb L] [K Street,] [Suburb L] and on non-school days as close to equal distance between the parties as possible.

Communication
That the non-resident parent be at liberty to video call the child via Google Meets App at 5.00 pm until 5.30 pm each Wednesday, Friday and Sunday with the non-resident parent to initiate the call provided however if the child requests extended time to communicate the parents shall facilitate the same not exceeding a further 30 minutes.
The parties shall communicate with one another via the ‘Our Family Wizard App’ save for in the event of an emergency where communication shall be by way of telephone call.

Medical
Each party shall forthwith inform the other by telephone call or Our Family Wizard App of illness or injuries sustained by [X] whilst in their care including information about her illness or injury and the details of the treating doctor or hospital.
Each party shall forthwith inform the other by telephone call or Our Family Wizard App of childhood illness or emergency the parent with care of the child contacts the other parent as soon as practicable.

...

Information Sharing
The parties shall keep each other informed in accordance with Order 20 of any change to their residential address email address and telephone numbers within 24

...

(As per the original)

I note that this is therefore a matter in which the mother herself seeks shared decision-making authority and substantially shared time, which, together with a number of the other orders sought by the mother, would entail extensive ongoing interactions and discussions between the parties.
It was submitted on behalf of the father that the mother's evidence does not rise to a level which would attract the discretion within section 102NA. Whilst, as I have said, some of the conduct alleged by the mother may fall within the definition of family violence in section 4AB of the Act, I am not satisfied that the evidence before the Court supports the likely existence of the matters to which the Family Violence and Cross-Examination of Parties Scheme is directed.
The present application was made extremely late in the proceedings. It is highly unlikely that it could be arranged and legal practitioners could thereafter be properly prepared in time for the trial on 11 February 2026 which is just over two weeks away, noting that the standard minimum notice period for an application is 12 weeks prior to trial.
This means that if the order were made as sought by the mother, either the mother would be unable to cross-examine the father at the trial, or, in the event that she applied for and persuaded the Court to grant an adjournment, then having regard to the Court’s present listing capacity, there would be a delay of many more months before the trial could be heard. Both alternatives would likely prejudice the mother.
An adjournment would also potentially undermine the best interests of the child and would have a negative impact on the resources of the Court and other matters awaiting trial dates, and there may well be a negative impact on the father of continuing to be engaged in litigation.
Furthermore, one of the issues to be determined at the trial is the child's schooling which she is due to commence in mid-2026, prior to any likely available adjourned trial date.
During the course of her submissions, the mother, to her credit, readily acknowledged that an adjournment would not be appropriate in these circumstances and was clear that she did not seek to delay the trial.
In all of the circumstances, I am not satisfied that this discretion should be exercised in favour of granting the order, and the Application in a Proceeding filed on 22 January 2026 is dismissed.
